text: Among elite runners with similar VO2max, running economy explains approximately 65% of the variation in 10 km race performance.
VO2max has a negligible correlation with 10 km race performance among elite runners of comparable ability (r = -0.12, not significant).
Running economy (steady-state VO2 at submaximal paces) is strongly correlated with 10 km performance in elite runners, with correlations of r = 0.79-0.83 across multiple paces.
Once VO2max reaches elite levels (~72 ml/kg/min), further improvements in race performance depend more on running economy than on increasing VO2max.
Elite male 10 km runners averaging 32.1 min race times consume approximately 44.7-55.9 ml/kg/min at submaximal paces of 241-295 m/min, representing 62-78% of their VO2max.
